Job approaches the question being debated. This was whether outward success indicates the true church and its genuine members, so that a person's loss of success proves them a hypocrite? They asserted this, but Job denied it. If they looked at him, they would see enough misery to warrant compassion, and their bold interpretations of this mysterious providence should become silent wonder.

Job says, Remarkable judgments sometimes come upon obvious sinners, but not always. Why is that so? This is God’s time of patience; and in some way, he uses the success of the wicked to serve his own purposes while it prepares them for ruin; but the main reason is because he will show there is another world. These successful sinners dismiss God and religion as if, because they have much of this world, they do not need to consider another. But religion is not a meaningless thing. If it seems that way to us, we can thank ourselves for resting on the surface of it. Job shows their foolishness.

Job had described the success of wicked people; in these verses he contrasts this with what his friends had maintained about their certain ruin in this life. He reconciles this to God’s holiness and justice. Even while they prosper, they are light and worthless, having no value with God or wise people. At the height of their splendor and power, there is only a small distance between them and ruin. Job attributes the difference that providence makes between one wicked person and another to God's wisdom. He is Judge of all the earth, and he will do what is right. The disparity between time and eternity is so vast that if hell is the final destiny of every sinner, it does not matter whether one goes there joyfully and another sorrowfully. If one wicked person dies in a palace and another in a dungeon, the worm that never dies and the fire that cannot be extinguished will be the same for them. Therefore, differences in this world are not worth worrying about.

Job opposes his friends’ opinion: That the wicked are certain to fall into visible and remarkable ruin, and only the wicked; on this principle they condemned Job as wicked. Look wherever you want, you will find that the punishment of sinners is intended more for the next world than for this one. The sinner is supposed to live with great power. The sinner will have a splendid funeral: a poor thing for anyone to be proud of. He will have an impressive monument. A valley with springs of water to keep the grass green was considered an honorable burial place among eastern people; but such things are empty distinctions. Death ends his success. It is little encouragement to die that others have died before us. What enables a person to die with true courage is remembering, with faith, that Jesus Christ died and was laid in the grave, not only before us, but for us. That he has gone ahead of us and died for us, who is alive and lives for us, is true comfort at the hour of death.
